Home Volunteer Yard Signs Fact Check Donate

Don't Take Our Word For It.
Read the Fine Print.

Every claim we make about El Cerrito's Measure C comes from the city's own documents, state law, and public data. Here are the receipts.

Primary source: City of El Cerrito §9212 Impact Report (Feb 2026) →

On This Page

    Fact

    Backers Said the Library Would Be $21 Million, But Changed Their Story Once They Got Their Tax on the Ballot

    While collecting signatures, the Yes campaign told residents the library would cost $21,214,000. Shortly after they collected signatures, they deleted their old website — but you can still see it via the Wayback Machine. Now they acknowledge it would cost $37.2 million — a 77% increase. That's before interest on 30 years of bond financing.

    Yes Campaign Website (c4psl.org/faq) — Since Deleted, Archived via Wayback Machine
    Yes campaign FAQ (c4psl.org) — archived via Wayback Machine, since deleted from their site ROM Cost Estimate — Griffin Structures (reissued Jan 2026) — $37.2M §9212 Impact Report, pp. 18, 25-26
    Fact

    Supporters Said This Would Be Affordable, But Wrote the Fine Print to Let the City Aggressively Raise Taxes Every Year Without a Vote

    If costs continue to spiral — up 77% from when they were collecting signatures — so will your tax bill.

    The ballot measure (Sec. 4.58.040.B) lets the city increase the tax each year by the greater of Bay Area CPI or California per capita income growth — whichever is higher, no vote required.

    §9212 Impact Report, Page 6 — The Escalator Language

    Over the last 30 years, this "greater of" formula has averaged 5.05% annually — nearly double normal inflation.

    5.05%
    30-year average annual increase permitted
    2.89%
    Bay Area CPI alone (normal inflation)
    24 of 30
    Years income growth was the higher measure
    Ballot measure text — Sec. 4.58.040(B) ABAG CPI Tool (BLS data) FRED — CA Per Capita Personal Income (BEA data)
    Fact

    The Cost to the Average Resident

    Based on the city's estimate of average home size (1,946 SF) and 5.05% historical escalation.

    $331
    Year 1
    $403
    Year 5
    $660
    Year 15
    $1,382
    Year 30

    The city's §9212 report (p. 18) uses an average residential home size of 1,946 SF and an initial tax of $331/year. At the 5.05% historical escalation rate, that grows to over $1,300 by year 30. Landlords will pass this cost to renters.

    §9212 Impact Report, p. 18 — average home size and initial tax
    Fact

    The City Is Allowed to Collect More Than What Is Needed for a Library

    Supporters say all the money will be spent on libraries, but they wrote the fine print to allow the city to raise extra revenue.

    The ballot measure (Sec. 4.58.040.C) caps the tax at 115% of the rate needed to cover library costs — meaning the city can collect 15% more than it actually needs in any given year.

    §9212 Impact Report, Page 6 — 115% Overcollection Language
    §9212 Impact Report, pp. 6–7 and 13 Ballot measure text — Sec. 4.58.040(C)
    Fact

    The Tax Will Blow a $797,000 Hole in the Budget After 10 Years

    The city's own chart (p. 19) shows operating costs covered for only the first 10 years — then dropping to zero. You're still paying the tax. It just stops covering the cost of running the library.

    §9212 Impact Report, Page 19 — The Fiscal Cliff

    "The estimated impact to the General Fund at that time could be between approximately $187,755 to $797,000 annually… with costs expected to escalate each year thereafter."

    — §9212 Impact Report, p. 20

    §9212 Impact Report, Page 20 — The Cost Gap
    §9212 Impact Report, pp. 19–20
    Fact

    Supporters and the City Promised a Senior Exemption, But the Fine Print Makes It Essentially Impossible

    The official ballot title and summary — written by the City Attorney — tells voters: "Seniors who own single-family residential parcels…would be exempt from the tax if they qualify for participation in either of two identified programs."

    It doesn't name the programs. It doesn't mention one hasn't been funded since 2008. It doesn't mention the other puts a lien on your house.

    — Revised Ballot Title and Summary

    Here's what the §9212 report actually says about those two programs:

    §9212 Impact Report, Page 9 — Senior Exemption Language (note: "Gonzales" misspelling)
    1

    The "Gonzales-Deukmejian-Petris" program — except the correct legal name is Gonsalves. They got the name wrong. The city's own report (p. 9) admits: "The program is not currently funded by the State." It hasn't been funded since 2008.

    2

    The Property Tax Postponement program — requires age 62+, household income ≤$55,181, at least 40% equity, no reverse mortgage, and the state puts a lien on your house. About 1,337 people participate statewide.

    The Measure H precedent. This is the same exemption language used in El Cerrito's 2019 Measure H (pool & rec tax). Under that identical language, zero seniors have ever qualified. The city still has no application form.

    Renters get nothing. The exemption only applies to property owners.

    Ballot Title and Summary — senior exemption claim §9212 Impact Report, p. 9 — misspelled name, "not currently funded" CA Rev & Tax Code §2615.6 — correct name "Gonsalves" LAO Report — PTAP suspended since 2008-09 State Controller — PTP program requirements LAO EconTax — PTP participation data (~1,337 statewide) ECCRG Blog — Measure H precedent, zero qualifications
    LEARN MORE ABOUT THE SENIOR EXEMPTION →
    Fact

    This Will Be Built at the BART Station

    Now they're pretending the library might go elsewhere. Don't believe them.

    Their campaign committee is literally called "Citizens for a Plaza Station Library." The BART site is embedded in the city's Strategic Plan 2024–2029, which calls for the city to "Create a downtown community hub anchored by the Plaza Transit-Oriented Development and a new library."

    City of El Cerrito Strategic Plan 2024–2029 ECCRG Blog — Committee name, op-eds, council support for BART site
    Fact

    The City Acknowledges That Renovating the Current Library Would Be Cheaper

    The city's own cost estimates (§9212 report, pp. 25-26) show the current site rebuild — even with an 86-stall parking garage the city doesn't require at BART — comes in cheaper:

    ScenarioCost
    Current site rebuild, 13,000 SF
    + 86-stall parking garage    		 $34.8M
    BART TOD, 20,000 SF
    no dedicated parking    		 $37.2M
    §9212 Impact Report, pp. 25–27
    Fact

    The City Is Holding the BART Site to a Different Standard

    Requiring no dedicated parking at BART, while saying the Stockton site would need an 86-spot parking garage at $59,800 a stall.

    §9212 Impact Report, Page 27 — Parking Costs

    The city included an 86-stall subterranean parking garage in the current site cost — $59,801 per stall — but the BART option has zero dedicated library parking. The BART library would rely on ~165 street spaces within a 5-minute walk.

    The city's rationale (p. 27): they only studied parking solutions for the BART site because that's the only option the Council directed staff to investigate. This structurally inflates the current site's cost in any comparison.

    §9212 Impact Report, pp. 25–27 City Library FAQ — street parking reliance ECCRG — no dedicated library parking at BART
